questions.question: Explain the need for cache memory in modern computer systems. Why is it placed between the CPU and main memory?
keyPoints: Processor speed is much faster than memory access time creating a bottleneck
Cache acts as a high-speed buffer storing frequently accessed data
Reduces average memory access time by keeping frequently used data close to CPU
Uses locality of reference principle to be effective

questions.question: Define temporal locality and spatial locality. How does cache memory exploit both these principles?
keyPoints: Temporal locality: same memory location accessed repeatedly in short time
Spatial locality: nearby memory locations accessed in short time
Cache fetches entire blocks (cache lines) exploiting spatial locality
Cached data remains available for repeated access exploiting temporal locality

questions.question: Explain the difference between write-through and write-back policies. Which one ensures better data consistency and why?
keyPoints: Write-through: updates both cache and main memory simultaneously
Write-back: updates cache first, main memory only on block replacement
Write-through ensures better consistency as memory always has latest data
Write-back is faster but risks data loss if system crashes before writing back

questions.question: Describe three cache mapping techniques with their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Direct mapping: simple hardware, fixed position, causes conflict misses
Fully associative: flexible placement, complex hardware, expensive comparators
Set-associative: compromise between the two, multiple ways per set
Choice depends on cost, performance requirements, and application patterns

questions.question: What is cache hit ratio? How does it affect the average memory access time? Derive the formula for average access time.
keyPoints: Hit ratio = hits / total accesses
Average access time = (hit ratio × cache access time) + (miss ratio × (cache + memory time))
Higher hit ratio means performance closer to cache speed
Miss penalty significantly impacts performance when hit ratio is low

questions.question: Explain LRU (Least Recently Used) cache replacement policy. Why is it considered effective?
keyPoints: LRU evicts the block that has not been accessed for the longest time
Based on temporal locality - recently used blocks likely to be used again
Hardware implementation can be complex for large caches
Effective for most applications but may not always be optimal

questions.question: A computer system has a cache access time of 10 ns and main memory access time of 200 ns. If the cache hit ratio is 90%, calculate the average memory access time. Also explain how increasing cache size affects hit ratio and access time, discussing the trade-offs involved.
keyPoints: Average access time calculation: 0.9 × 10 + 0.1 × 210 = 9 + 21 = 30 ns
Without cache would be 200 ns, so cache provides significant speedup
Larger cache: higher hit ratio but slower access time per cache line search
Optimal cache size balances hit rate improvement against increased access latency
Cost is also a factor - larger caches are more expensive

questions.question: Explain the three levels of cache (L1, L2, L3) typically found in modern processors. Include details about their size, speed, location, and why this hierarchical design is used.
keyPoints: L1: smallest (16-64KB), fastest (1-2 cycle access), closest to CPU core
L2: larger (256KB-1MB), slower (10-20 cycles), may be shared or per-core
L3: largest (several MB), slowest (30-50 cycles), shared across cores
Hierarchy provides balance of speed, size, and cost
Smaller and faster caches closer to CPU, larger slower ones further away
Multi-level caching reduces average memory access time significantly

questions.question: Describe the concept of cache coherence in multiprocessor systems. What problems arise without it, and briefly explain one protocol used to maintain coherence.
keyPoints: Multiple processors have independent caches sharing main memory
Without coherence: one CPU may read stale data while another has updated it
This leads to incorrect program execution and data inconsistencies
MESI protocol uses four states: Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid
On write: invalidate other caches or update them
Ensures all processors see same data for same memory location

questions.question: A direct-mapped cache has 64 cache lines and each line (block) stores 16 bytes. For the following memory addresses (in decimal), determine which cache line each would map to: 100, 260, 564, 1024. Show your working.
keyPoints: Number of cache lines = 64, so index requires 6 bits (2^6 = 64)
Block size = 16 bytes = 2^4 bytes, so offset requires 4 bits
For address 100: 100 ÷ 16 = 6 remainder 4, so block 6 maps to line 6
For address 260: 260 ÷ 16 = 16 remainder 4, block 16 maps to line 16
For address 564: 564 ÷ 16 = 35 remainder 4, block 35 maps to line 35
For address 1024: 1024 ÷ 16 = 64 remainder 0, block 64 maps to line 0 (64 mod 64 = 0)
